sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.content: Differentiated thyroid carcinomas usually have an excellent prognosis with 10-year survival rates exceeding 90% to 95%. In contrast, anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) is almost uniformly lethal with a median survival of approximately 5 months and a 1-year survival rate of approximately 20%. Because differentiated thyroid carcinomas represent more than 95% of all cases, most thyroid carcinoma deaths are from papillary, follicular, and oncocytic carcinomas. In 2024, approximately 2,170 cancer deaths were estimated to occur among persons with thyroid carcinoma in the United States.

factors.detail: most important prognostic variable for thyroid cancer mortality, with a cutoff of 55 years predicting the presence of distant metastasis
linear relationship between tumor size and recurrence or cancer-specific mortality
aggressive variants including tall-cell, columnar-cell, hobnail, and diffuse sclerosing have worse prognosis
associated with more aggressive disease and higher recurrence
recurrence rates two times higher with locally invasive tumors
especially associated with worse outcomes in older patients
principal cause of death from differentiated thyroid carcinomas
when combined with TERT promoter mutation, associated with poor prognosis
associated with lower disease-free survival; HR 3.37; 95% CI 2.54–4.48; P < .00001
risk of death from cancer approximately twice as high in individuals assigned male at birth

factors.factor: RET codon status in medullary carcinoma
factors.detail: exon 16/M918T mutations associated with more aggressive disease

factors.factor: Calcitonin/CEA doubling times in medullary carcinoma
factors.detail: rapid calcitonin and CEA doubling times are predictive of more aggressive disease
poorly differentiated and anaplastic have markedly worse outcomes
